title: Fluid mosaic membranes
paperName: Paper 1 Multiple Choice & Paper 2 AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Cambridge 9700 Paper 2 — Fluid mosaic membranes (4.1). A-Level Notes diagram-backed lesson with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Describe the fluid mosaic model, including the arrangement of its components.
Explain the specific functions of phospholipids, cholesterol, and various proteins within cell membranes.
Discuss how the fluidity and mosaic nature of membranes enable their diverse cellular functions.

simpleExplanation.title: Unpacking the Cell's Skin
simpleExplanation.summary: Imagine a cell's outer layer, not as a rigid wall, but a dynamic, flexible barrier. This 'skin' is the fluid mosaic membrane, a bustling crowd of tiny molecules constantly moving, controlling what enters and leaves the cell. It's vital for cell survival and communication.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a busy fairground floating on water. The water is the fluid phospholipid bilayer, allowing movement. Stalls, rides, and people (representing proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates) are scattered throughout, performing specific jobs while drifting and changing positions.
steps: Visualise the phospholipid bilayer structure, understanding its hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions.
Identify and describe the role of each key component: phospholipids, cholesterol, intrinsic/extrinsic proteins, glycoproteins, and glycolipids.
Understand *why* it's called 'fluid' (due to phospholipid and protein movement) and 'mosaic' (due to the scattered arrangement of proteins).
Connect the structural features of the membrane directly to its functions, such as selective permeability, cell signalling, and transport.

sections.content: Every living cell is encased in a remarkable structure: the **cell surface membrane**. Far from a simple, static boundary, it's a dynamic, intricate barrier known as the **fluid mosaic membrane**. This model, proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972, explains how the membrane's components are arranged and how they work together to control what enters and leaves the cell, facilitating communication and maintaining cellular integrity. Let's delve into its fascinating structure and function. Key points align with **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** 9700 Biology chapter **4** (topic **4.1**). Notes and diagrams from **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** chapter **4**, topic **4.1** (1 pages).
A-Level Notes — topic content
1) Phospholipids Image: https://courses.lumenlearning.com/

• phospholipids are arranged so that hydrophobic, non- polar tails do not face water.
• Water is on both the intracellular and extracellular sides.
• therefore, tails point inwards, and hydrophilic heads face the aqueous medium Image: https://www.chegg.com/ Membrane fluidity Membrane fluidity refers to the viscosity of the lipid bilayer of a cell membrane.
• Membrane fluidity is affected by:.
1) tail length – longer the tail, the less fluid the membrane
2) saturation of fatty acid – the more unsaturated they are, the more fluid the membrane. Thi
• s is as unsaturated fatty acid tails are bent and fit together more loosely.
3) cholesterol

• regulates the fluidity of membrane.
• at low temperatures, cholesterol increases the fluidity of the membrane preventing it from being too rigid, this is because it prevents close packing of phospholipid tails.
• at high temperatures, cholesterol decreases the fluidity of membrane and stabilises the cell 2,.
3) Glycolipids and glycoproteins Lipid and protein molecules on the outer surfaces of cell me

• mbrane have carbohydrate chains attached to them forming glycolipids and glycoproteins These carbohydrate chains projecting out like antennae:.
• stabilise the membrane structure by forming hydrogen bonds with water molecules surrounding the cell.
• glycocalyx – sugary cell coating formed by carbohydrate chains.
• act as receptor molecules: Þ signalling receptors – recognise messenger molecules like hormones and neurotransmitters Þ endocytosis – bind to molecule to be engulfed by membrane.
• act as cell markers/antigens allowing cell-cell recognition.
4) Proteins

• proteins that are found embedded within the membrane.
• can be present inside or outside of the cell membrane i.e., intracellular, and extracellular.
• may be found in inner layer, outer layer or spanning the whole membrane (these are transmembrane proteins).
• extracellular peripheral proteins – communication, receptors, and recognition proteins.
• helps in movement in and out of cell.
• intracellular peripheral proteins- structural support, attached to the cytoskeleton of the cell.
The Fluid Mosaic Model: A Dynamic Blueprint

The fluid mosaic model describes the cell membrane as a constantly moving, flexible structure. The 'fluid' aspect refers to the constant motion of its components, primarily the phospholipids and proteins. The 'mosaic' aspect describes the varied, scattered arrangement of proteins embedded within or attached to the lipid bilayer, much like tiles in a mosaic artwork. This dynamic nature is crucial for the membrane's diverse roles.
Key Components and Their Roles
The cell surface membrane is primarily composed of lipids and proteins, with some carbohydrates. Each component plays a vital role:
Phospholipids

These are the fundamental building blocks, forming a **phospholipid bilayer**. Each phospholipid molecule has a **hydrophilic head** (attracted to water, facing outwards towards the aqueous cytoplasm and extracellular fluid) and two **hydrophobic tails** (repelled by water, facing inwards towards each other). This arrangement creates a barrier that is selectively permeable, primarily allowing small, lipid-soluble molecules to pass through. The phospholipids are constantly moving, undergoing lateral diffusion, rotation, and flexion, contributing significantly to the membrane's fluidity and self-sealing capability.
Cholesterol

Found exclusively in **animal cell membranes**, cholesterol molecules are nestled between the hydrophobic tails of phospholipids. They act as a **fluidity buffer**. At higher temperatures, cholesterol reduces membrane fluidity by restricting phospholipid movement. At lower temperatures, it prevents the phospholipids from packing too closely, thereby stopping the membrane from becoming too rigid or solidifying. This stabilises the membrane's mechanical strength and permeability over a range of temperatures.
Proteins

Proteins are the workhorses of the membrane, performing a vast array of functions. They are categorised based on their position:

*   **Intrinsic (Integral) Proteins:** These are firmly embedded within the lipid bilayer. Some are **transmembrane proteins**, spanning the entire membrane and protruding on both sides (e.g., channel proteins, carrier proteins). Others are embedded only on one side. They are crucial for transport, acting as enzymes, or serving as receptors for cell signalling.
*   **Extrinsic (Peripheral) Proteins:** These are loosely attached to the surface of the membrane or to intrinsic proteins, often on the cytoplasmic side. They can be involved in cell recognition, structural support, or as enzymes.
Glycoproteins and Glycolipids

These are carbohydrate chains (oligosaccharides) covalently bonded to proteins (forming **glycoproteins**) or lipids (forming **glycolipids**) on the outer surface of the cell membrane. Together, they form the **glycocalyx**, a sugary coat that is unique to each cell type and individual. The glycocalyx is vital for cell-cell recognition, cell adhesion, and acting as receptor sites for hormones or neurotransmitters.

sections.question: Explain how the structure of the fluid mosaic membrane contributes to its selective permeability. (6 marks)
sections.solution:
The selective permeability of the fluid mosaic membrane arises from the combined actions of its components:

1.  **Phospholipid Bilayer:** The hydrophobic fatty acid tails form a non-polar, lipid-soluble interior, which acts as the primary barrier. This restricts the passage of large molecules, charged ions, and highly polar substances, allowing only small, non-polar molecules (e.g., oxygen, carbon dioxide) and some small polar molecules (e.g., water via osmosis) to pass directly through.

2.  **Intrinsic Proteins (Channels and Carriers):** For substances that cannot cross the lipid bilayer (e.g., ions, glucose, amino acids), specific intrinsic proteins provide pathways. Channel proteins form hydrophilic pores allowing specific ions to pass, while carrier proteins bind to specific molecules and change shape to facilitate their transport across the membrane. This ensures only specific substances are transported.

3.  **Overall Fluidity:** The dynamic nature of the membrane, due to the movement of phospholipids and proteins, allows for processes like endocytosis and exocytosis. These bulk transport mechanisms enable the uptake or release of larger particles or entire substances that would otherwise be unable to cross the membrane, but this is still a *controlled* and selective process by the cell.

sections.content: When describing membrane fluidity, focus on the *movement* of phospholipids (lateral diffusion, rotation, flexion) and proteins. Avoid simply saying it's 'not rigid'. For 'mosaic', emphasise the *distribution* of embedded and surface proteins. Always link the structural features directly to their *function* and refer to specific components.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What is the primary function of the fluid mosaic membrane in cells?
faq.a: The primary function is to regulate the movement of substances into and out of the cell, maintaining homeostasis. It also plays critical roles in cell signalling, adhesion, and recognition. You can deepen your understanding by exploring our free Cambridge Biology 9700 course content and practising with past papers.

faq.q: How does temperature affect the fluidity of a cell membrane?
faq.a: At higher temperatures, phospholipids gain kinetic energy, increasing membrane fluidity. At lower temperatures, fluidity decreases, potentially making the membrane too rigid. Cholesterol, found in animal cells, helps buffer these changes, maintaining optimal fluidity. Our premium lessons dive deeper into these effects and provide past paper questions to test your knowledge.

faq.q: Why is the term 'mosaic' used to describe cell membranes?
faq.a: The term 'mosaic' refers to the scattered and irregular arrangement of various proteins embedded within or attached to the phospholipid bilayer, much like tiles in a mosaic artwork. These proteins are not uniformly distributed but form a diverse pattern essential for specific functions. Learn more about membrane proteins and their roles in our free 9700 Biology revision series, complete with detailed explanations and practice questions.
